title: Basic Greetings
objectives: Recognize and use the core greeting and farewell expressions, matched correctly to time of day and formality.
grammarFocus: Introduction to être: je suis / tu es, used only in the fixed pattern 'Je suis... / Tu es...'
vocabularyFocus: bonjour, bonsoir, salut, au revoir, à bientôt, bonne nuit, madame, monsieur

warmUp.content: Think about how you greet people in your own language. Do you say something different in the morning than at night? Do you greet a stranger the same way you greet a close friend? Keep these questions in mind — French makes some of the same distinctions, and a few new ones.

explanation.content: French greetings change depending on two things: the time of day and how well you know the person. Bonjour ('hello' / 'good day') is the all-purpose daytime greeting, used with anyone from morning until early evening. Bonsoir ('good evening') replaces bonjour once evening arrives. Salut ('hi' / 'bye') is casual and only used with people you know well. Au revoir ('goodbye') works in any situation, formal or informal. À bientôt ('see you soon') is warm and casual. Bonne nuit ('good night') is used only when someone is going to bed, not as a general evening farewell. Madame and Monsieur are polite titles added after bonjour/bonsoir when addressing someone you don't know well.

vocabulary.usageNote: Only said when someone is actually going to bed.

grammar.explanation: This lesson introduces only a small, fixed piece of the verb être ('to be'): the phrase pattern Je suis... ('I am...') used to state your name. The full conjugation of être is built gradually across this chapter.
grammar.usage: This pattern is commonly used right after a greeting, when meeting someone for the first time: Bonjour, je suis Claire.

commonMistakes.why: No preposition is needed before a name with être.
commonMistakes.tip: Just Je suis + name, nothing in between.

commonMistakes.why: Proper names are always capitalized in French, exactly as in English.

questions.prompt: Bonjour, __________. (complete with 'Je suis' + your own name)
questions.explanation: Je suis + name is the fixed pattern taught in this lesson for stating your name after a greeting.

questions.prompt: Salut, __________ ! (complete with 'Je suis' + your own name)
questions.explanation: The same Je suis + name pattern works after any greeting, formal or informal.

reading.title: Deux Rencontres (Two Encounters)
reading.text:
Le matin, au bureau :
Camille : Bonjour, Madame Petit !
Madame Petit : Bonjour ! Je suis Madame Petit.
Camille : Je suis Camille. Enchantée !

Le soir, dans la rue :
Hugo : Salut, Léa !
Léa : Salut, Hugo ! Ça va ?
Hugo : Ça va, merci. Bonne nuit, à bientôt !
Léa : À bientôt !
reading.translation:
In the morning, at the office:
Camille: Hello, Madame Petit!
Madame Petit: Hello! I am Madame Petit.
Camille: I am Camille. Nice to meet you!

In the evening, on the street:
Hugo: Hi, Léa!
Léa: Hi, Hugo! How's it going?
Hugo: Good, thanks. Good night, see you soon!
Léa: See you soon!

questions.prompt: What time of day does the first conversation happen?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly labels the scene 'Le matin, au bureau.'

questions.prompt: Which greeting does Camille use with Madame Petit?
questions.explanation: It's morning and a formal workplace context, so Camille uses Bonjour.

questions.prompt: Why does Hugo use salut instead of bonjour?
questions.correctAnswer: Because he knows Léa well and it's an informal situation.
questions.explanation: Salut is reserved for people you know well, unlike the all-purpose bonjour.

questions.prompt: What does Léa say at the very end?
questions.explanation: Léa's final line in the dialogue is 'À bientôt !'

listening.title: Au Café
listening.transcript:
Serveur : Bonsoir, Madame !
Cliente : Bonsoir, Monsieur.
Serveur : Vous êtes Madame Lambert ?
Cliente : Oui, je suis Madame Lambert.
Serveur : Merci ! Au revoir, bonne soirée.
Cliente : Au revoir !
listening.translation:
Waiter: Good evening, Madam!
Customer: Good evening, Sir.
Waiter: Are you Madame Lambert?
Customer: Yes, I am Madame Lambert.
Waiter: Thank you! Goodbye, have a good evening.
Customer: Goodbye!

questions.prompt: The conversation happens in the morning.
questions.explanation: The waiter says 'Bonsoir', which signals evening, not morning.

questions.prompt: The customer's name is Madame Lambert.
questions.explanation: She confirms this directly: 'Oui, je suis Madame Lambert.'

questions.prompt: The waiter says salut at the end.
questions.explanation: He says 'Au revoir', the standard goodbye, not the informal salut.

speaking.guidedActivity: Practice saying bonjour and bonsoir aloud, paying attention to the difference in mouth position between the open 'oh' sound in bonjour and the rounder 'wah' sound in bonsoir.
speaking.roleplay: Person A greets Person B as a stranger in the morning, using Madame/Monsieur. Person B replies and introduces themselves with Je suis.... Both say goodbye with au revoir.
speaking.pronunciationTip: The 'r' in bonjour and au revoir is made in the back of the throat — softer than an English 'r.' Don't worry about perfecting it yet; just get comfortable attempting it.

writing.task: Write a 2–3 sentence mini-dialogue in which you greet someone in the evening, introduce yourself, and say goodbye.
writing.modelAnswer: Bonsoir, Madame. Je suis Julien. Au revoir, à bientôt !
checklist: Used a greeting that matches evening time (bonsoir, not bonjour).

questions.prompt: Which greeting is correct at 9:00 PM?
questions.explanation: 9:00 PM is evening, so bonsoir is correct; bonne nuit is reserved for bedtime specifically.

questions.prompt: Match the expression to its use.

pairs.right: Said only when going to bed

correctAnswer.right: Said only when going to bed

questions.explanation: Each expression maps to exactly one register/usage as taught in this lesson.
A polite daytime greeting with a title takes bonjour.
A natural exchange opens with a greeting, then a self-introduction, then a farewell.

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain when you would use salut instead of bonjour.
questions.correctAnswer: Salut is used with people you know well, in casual situations — never with strangers or in formal contexts.
questions.explanation: This checks understanding of the formality distinction central to this lesson.

miniReview.content: You can now greet someone appropriately by time of day, choose between formal and informal greetings, introduce yourself with Je suis, and say goodbye correctly.

selfAssessment: I can say hello appropriately for morning, afternoon, or evening.
I know when to use salut versus bonjour.
I can introduce myself using Je suis.
I can say goodbye in more than one way.
